svg.text: IPv4 · IPv6 · TCP · UDP · DNS · HTTP · TLS · QUIC · and beyond
US DoD ARPA connects 4 universities: UCLA,
Stanford, UCSB, Utah. First message sent: "LO"
Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn publish "A Protocol for
how disparate networks can talk to each other.
The blueprint that becomes IPv4 and TCP.
of Internet Transmission Control Program." First
formal definition of the reliable byte-stream protocol.
TCP and IP separated into two distinct protocols.
IPv4 handles addressing + routing; TCP handles
reliability. This split enables UDP to exist.
Header: 20 bytes min · TTL · Protocol · Checksum
Jon Postel writes RFC 791. Still powers the internet.
UDP: fire-and-forget, minimal overhead, no ack.
from NCP to TCP/IP. "Flag Day" of the internet.
Cerf and Kahn's internet becomes real. ~600 hosts.
Paul Mockapetris invents DNS — maps hostnames
to IP addresses, replacing HOSTS.TXT file.
Root servers, TLDs, recursive resolvers born.
Tim Berners-Lee at CERN proposes the World Wide
Web. HTTP/0.9 — single GET, no headers.
HTML + URLs + HTTP: the web's three pillars.
NSF lifts ban on commercial internet traffic.
WWW goes live to the public. Gopher, FTP, IRC.
Internet grows from ~600k to millions of hosts.
Netscape invents SSL to secure web transactions.
SSL 1.0 never released. SSL 2.0 ships in 1994.
Enables e-commerce. Becomes TLS 30 years later.
HTTP/1.0 adds headers, status codes, methods.
One TCP connection per request — very slow.
IETF warns IPv4's 4.29 billion addresses will run out.
Drives urgency for IPv6 design. CIDR deployed.
No NAT needed · Built-in IPSec · Auto-config (SLAAC)
Simplified header · Flow labels · No broadcast
Persistent connections · chunked encoding · Host header.
Dominates the web for nearly 20 years.
IETF standardises TLS 1.0 based on SSL 3.0.
Fixes SSL vulnerabilities. HTTPS becomes viable.
Foundation of all secure internet traffic today.
6bone test network. ISPs begin IPv6 trials.
Only 0.2% of traffic — IPv4 NAT too convenient.
WebSocket enables full-duplex comms over TCP.
Starts as HTTP upgrade. RFC 6455 in 2011.
Real-time apps: chat, gaming, live dashboards.
IANA allocates last /8 IPv4 blocks. Feb 3, 2011.
RIPE NCC (Europe) runs out in 2012. APNIC 2011.
IPv6 transition becomes urgent. World IPv6 Day.
Google, Facebook, Yahoo, Bing permanently enable
IPv6. ISPs begin dual-stack rollouts globally.
(HPACK) · server push. Based on Google's SPDY.
Removes RC4, MD5, SHA-1, 3DES, export ciphers.
ECDHE mandatory — Perfect Forward Secrecy always on.
0-RTT session resumption. Now default in all CDNs.
Google's QUIC becomes IETF standard. Runs over
UDP, not TCP. Multiplexed streams. TLS built-in.
No head-of-line blocking at transport layer.
Google, Cloudflare, Fastly deploy at scale.
25%+ of web traffic uses HTTP/3 by 2023.
Google reports 45%+ of users reach it via IPv6.
India, US, Germany, Belgium at 60–80% IPv6.
DoH (DNS over HTTPS) and DoT widening.
Post-quantum cryptography in TLS under development.
IPv4 vs IPv6 — Side-by-Side Deep Dive
No — every device gets public IP
No connection, no ack, no order
RFC 791 (IPv4) · RFC 8200 (IPv6) · RFC 793 (TCP) · RFC 768 (UDP) · RFC 9000 (QUIC) · RFC 9114 (HTTP/3)
IETF · IANA · ICANN · Jon Postel · Vint Cerf · Bob Kahn · Tim Berners-Lee · Network Spy Academy
